speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Regina Clipper, maiden name Rosenzweig, on May 17, 1995 in Long Beach, New York, USA, in English. Interviewer, Juliet Halpern. I am Juliet Halpern, interviewing Regina Clipper, May 17, 1995 in Long Beach, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Regina Rosensweig Clipper. I reversed it. I was born in Poland, January 2, 1924. We lived there till I was about one and a half years old, and we moved to Germany, to Dusseldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: What town were you in in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Kielce-- it's between Kielce and Warsaw. When we arrived in Dusseldorf, my father got a job in my uncle's wallpaper and paint factory. He designed wallpaper, and then he made the books that people pick their wallpaper from. I went to kindergarten-- not like here, just nursery school. And it was nice. Then I went to school, started school, and I liked it a lot. I had a very good teacher. It was a lovely school. And we were happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a special school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a public school. But it was a very good school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was sick a lot because as a teenager, she had contracted encephalitis, sleeping sickness. And she-- she felt ill a lot of time. We had a, a maid that came in every day. And that was a lot of problem because my mother was in bed or in, in the hospital. It was problematic. My father worked not too far away, so he could come for lunch home. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only child till 1930. My brother was born in Dusseldorf on August 15, 1930. His name was Joachim. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- my mother was sometimes a little better, sometimes a little worse. In 1932-- but life was nice. We belonged to a temple-- not the big German Jewish temple, but a smaller temple, because my parents came from Poland. And there was not the biggest friendship between the German Jews and the Polish Jews. But we had a very nice life. And then it started to become difficult. Anti-Semitism rose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an extended family? Did your mother and father have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an uncle-- no. I had an uncle, my mother's brother, who owned the factory. And they had-- he was married and had two children, but they were already almost adults. Their son was 13. His name was Wolfgang. And my cousin Willitrud was 18 or 19. And this cousin Willitrud eventually brought me to America, and lives next door to my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were in Poland. My father's parents were in Poland. My father came from a family of eight. And one son was in Palestine. He was a biologist. And he also was-- at that time, there was no Haganah, but later he became the chief of the Haganah for European affairs. He was a professor, and he changed his name to Chaim ben Menachim. The other children lived in Radom. One lived in, in Lodz, whom I never met. But the other sisters and brothers lived in Radom. We got together. Every Saturday we went to-- but that was already when we came back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1932, one day-- it was, I think, in, in October or November-- my father came home all bloody. He was beaten up by the Nazis, the Hitlerjugend. And he came home and he said, we got to go. We have to leave here. My uncle had left already. He went to Holland. And we went then also. And it was new. We didn't know the language. And we took very little along. We just went. Even though Hitler wasn't in power yet, there were a lot of disturbances and a lot of beatings. And my father just didn't want to wait again to be beaten up. We went to Holland in December of '32. We got an apartment. And my father worked at the same-- in the same kind of factory that he worked before. And I was in enrolled in a German school in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father thought it would-- I didn't know the language, so it would be easier for me to go and learn the curriculum and learn Dutch at the same time. It was a very nice school, very good school. And I was tested, and I was-- skipped a class. And I had a wonderful teacher. And one day, they got a visitor from Germany, a SS man-- SR man-- with the brown uniforms and the red arm band. And we had to-- the school had to perform-- it was an assembly. He researched what was being taught there. And we went up on the roof to a theater in the school, and we had-- each class had to perform for him. One girl played the piano. Some other's danced. There was a choir, some readings. And my teacher decided that I should recite a very famous poem. Well, I did. I went onto the stage and recited this poem and-- very fiery. And the SS man-- after I finished, everybody was applauding. And he came down to the stage, and he shook my hand. And I very nicely curtsied. And then he turned to the audience and he said, now here you can see what a pure Aryan child can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he turned to me and he asked me for my name, and I told him my name is Regina Rosenzweig. And he got furious. He didn't say anything, but he got wet in the face. And my teacher was very reprimanded. So isn't it ironic? It's really ironic that people can be so-- educated people-- because the Germans are educated-- could be so stupid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in school, and I liked school. I walked every morning. Although my mother gave me money for the trolley car, but I rather walked and spent the money on renting a bicycle on the weekend. In 1935, we got notice from the Dutch government that unless we are self-employed or had a big capital in the bank, we could not stay in Holland. We had to leave. And we did not have a big capital in the bank, and my father was working for someone else. So we tried to come to the United States. But being that we were Polish quota, and there was a quota system, we couldn't come in. Then we tried to go to Palestine, and the British wouldn't let us in. Then my parents decided, being that we had some family in Poland, we should go back to Poland. In January of 19-- the end of January 1935, we arrived in Poland. And I wasn't happy. It was strange to me. I didn't speak Polish. I didn't-- it was so bitter cold. I, I just didn't like it. And we stayed with family. First we stayed with my, my father's brother for a few weeks. We couldn't get an apartment. Then we stayed with my mother's sister for a while until we got a place to live. And times were very bad, and my father could get little work. And my mother didn't feel well, and I became the caretaker. I couldn't go to school because the Polish government has a policy. If you don't speak Polish, you can't enter the school system. So my mother hired a tutor for me to teach Polish and also curriculum-- geography and math, and so on. It was-- It was not a nice country. I was used to being in Holland, which is a wonderful country. But that was the way it was. In May or April of 1935-- we were just a few months there-- there was a pogrom, not in Radom, but on the outskirts, about 20 miles away from Radom. It was Passover, Easter, and they killed a lot of people, a lot of Jewish people. And that had a terrible effect on all of us because we really did not expect a thing like this to happen in a democratic country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that apparently is the way it is, and so you, you have to-- if you can't go anyplace else, you've got to be there. We then moved to a different apartment in the same building where in aunts lived. And there, we had a little closer contact. My mother's sister-- she had two sisters living in the same apartment house. So we, we had cousins and aunts and uncles, and life was a little nicer. I didn't have to be the caretaker exclusively. My aunt was there, and my other aunt was there. So it was better. In 1939 in September, the war broke out, and we were bombed in, in, in a few days. And at the first time that you experience such a thing, it's devastating. It didn't bomb our house, but next to us on the same street. And, and then we thought-- then Warsaw fell in six weeks, I think, and the Germans moved into Poland. First thing they did, we had to register. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this point, you are what? About 12 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I was-- from '35 to '39, there were years I-- oh, yeah. I forgot. I was learning dressmaking. My uncle was a tailor, and he-- I asked him if I could learn something, so I was learning dressmaking. But that didn't work out too well. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the Germans moved in-- moved-- I was 15, '39. I was 14 and a 1/2. And the Germans moved in. And we-- first we had to register that we were Jews and wear an armband. That was in 1940 on the beginning. And then we-- they took us for slave labor, my father and me. My brother was only 10 years old at time. And the same-- every day, we had to go to a center, and they took us to work. My father was painting. He was a professional painter and wallpaper, and the Germans are very clean. They want everything nice and pretty. So my father spoke German, so he worked for them there. Until he got it, he, he worked on the streets and cleaned the streets. But then they decided that being that he is a painter, he should paint for them. And me with other girls, they send onto snow shoveling, street cleaning, airport-- the runways, cleaning. Finally, they took us to a factory, like a giant garage, where the Germans repaired their tanks and their cars and jeeps, and all kinds of vehicles. And that was Heeres-Kraftfahr-Park. There we got a job, and every day we had to do the same thing. They put two girls-- a girl named Lola--

speaker: interviewer
text: Heeres-Kraft means--

speaker: interviewee
text: Army, kraft is strength vehicles. They repaired them there, so we had to shovel the coal, and we had to clean the garages, and all kinds of things, whatever they told us to do. I even peeled potatoes in the kitchen, which was very good because I had something to eat. And then they sent two girls to a house where officers and mechanics that came from, from Mercedes factory to repair all this in a big building about six stories that we had to clean the bathrooms, the kitchens, the staircases, and do the laundry. And we did this, and sometimes the civilians were not too bad. They gave us a piece of bread or, or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you experiencing any kind of abuse at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in there. Not in there. But we had to be very careful because there was an SS officer in charge. But later, I found out that he wasn't so bad either. We worked in the basement in a cellar to clean pot-- to, to select potatoes-- they shouldn't rot-- or carrots. And they had a big supply of coal to, to burn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told my brother, if he could come with a sled-- it was out of the ghetto-- but he was a little boy, and he was kind of reddish-blond and blue eyes. So he came with his sled to this house. In the back was a window on the side of the house, so nobody could really see it. And I went down through that window. I gave him a sack of coal and a sack of potatoes-- not a sack, but a-- and he brought it back into the ghetto. So we had some-- because I couldn't take it home because they searched. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the ghetto became smaller. And-- oh, this was in 1941. All of this happened in '41 and in the beginning of '42. And whatever I could-- sometimes they gave me something to take home, and they let me out from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the ghetto in Radom that you were living in.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom. In Radom, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had not always lived in the ghetto there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the beginning we did not live in the ghetto. The begin-- the ghetto first started in the end of '40. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that area walled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. Once we were in the ghetto, it was walled in. But, you know, if you know the town and you know the area, you can somehow manage, manage to steal yourself out. You remove the band and pray to God no should-- nobody should find you. And then we got some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have friends amongst the non-Jewish community in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. None. I didn't know anybody. I, I knew Jewish girls and boys, and belonged to the Ja-- Jabotinsky organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a Jewish Zionist organization. And we met. But in the ghetto, we couldn't do that anymore. We couldn't get together because they divided the city. One ghetto was on, on one side of the town, and one was on the other side of the town, so we didn't see each other. In-- in 19-- I still worked in the [NON-ENGLISH] constantly, every day-- sometimes in this building, sometimes in that building. In August, beginning of August of 19-- of '42, we were told that we have to come to work then and then, from the Germans told us. It was an odd day. I think it-- I don't remember what kind of a day. So to this place, not to any other. So we went. I worked. I had to go. And in the evening, when we were supposed to go home, they closed the gates on us. They wouldn't let us go. There were quite a few people there. And I said, but I have to go home to my parents. Well, you go home tomorrow. There are going to be some, some maneuvers there. You stay here. And we stayed. And the following day, we found out that they had sent away everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no-- you had no inkling of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent away everybody-- my parents, my grandparents, my uncles, my, my, my aunt, all the cousins. One uncle was shot. They told me later. And that was-- then I was alone. And I couldn't understand. I said to the German, the hauptmann, why wouldn't you let me go home? I wanted to go with my parents. He said, well, you're young. You should live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, we went into a smaller ghetto were about three streets, the, the, the the survivors of the-- and there was-- Do you remember how it was when you found out about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was absolute-- first I was numb. I couldn't-- I wouldn't believe it. Because I said, but my father worked for the Germans. And he-- well, one friend that stayed that was left said, your father was the first they took. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [CRYING] so then they, they put us in the smaller ghetto. And we still went to work for the [NON-ENGLISH]. In the morning, out. In the evening, back. We got some ration cards. And what we didn't steal on the-- on-- at work-- we, we didn't have-- never got enough to eat. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out where they had all been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us on the wagons to Treblinka. They told us. And we didn't know much about Treblinka. We didn't know much about Auschwitz either, you know? We didn't read any newspaper or listen to, to, to a radio. Just by word of mouth that somebody mentioned something, then it traveled around. And you know when--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no radios in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took the radios away in the very beginning of the occupation. When you're in this position and you want to believe that they were in another camp or in another ghetto. And we didn't know what was Treblinka. Maybe it was another camp. You don't want to believe the-- the worst. Otherwise, you just can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that time-- it was from August till January-- January? Yeah. They rounded up, again, people from the ghetto, the ones that weren't working. The [NON-ENGLISH] closed there, so I wasn't working. They rounded us up, and we went on the march. It was bitter cold and snow, about, oh, 30 kilometer to this village, a small town that had a lot of railroad tracks because there used to be a lot of factories there. And we walked there, and the older people, whoever could find a wagon, took the babies on the wagon. And, and we got there. So it was a leather factory where they cure the leather. And that's where we-- they put us on, on straw. We were sitting there. And they told us we are going to go to another camp, and the trains are coming tomorrow. So I was sitting with a friend, a young girl. And I said, we've got to get out of here. They're going-- they're not going to send us to another camp. We have to get back. So early in the morning, we snuck out, and we walked back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was daylight, so we got stopped. Well, we had these babushkas on and old clothes. We went to the-- on the road, so we were stopped by several people-- Polish farmers, a Polish policeman. And there were also Germans that drove back and forth in their big cars. They never paid attention to us because we didn't look Jewish, what, whatever that means. But the Polish stopped us. They stopped us, and we gave them whatever-- she had a pair of earrings. I had a little necklace, a little ring, a silver ring. And he took it, and he let us go. They let us go. We arrived at about three or four blocks from the ghetto, and it became dusk. It became dark. And we hid in the shed so nobody should see us. That's where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's pause now and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.
